speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is March 27, 1997. The survivor is James Krauss, born Imre Krausz. The interviewer is Adele Abolafia. We are in Forest Hills, New York, in the United States of America, and the language is English. My name is Adele Abolafia, A-D-E-L-E, A-B-O-L-A-F-I-A. Today is March 27, 1997. I am interviewing James Krauss for the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. We are in Forest Hills, New York, in the United States of America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mr. Krauss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please state and spell your name? Your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is James Krauss, K-R-A-U-S-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth was Imre Krauss, K-R-A-U-S-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you state the date that you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born May 2, 1930, in Budapest 19-- yeah, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My nickname was Immy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what life was like for you in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're talking about--

speaker: interviewer
text: As a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say life was fairly normal, considering, before the war. We-- we saw-- we had the usual difficulties, which we didn't consider that difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of-- and what kind were the usual difficulties that you may--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, occasionally you got beat up. But it was not that difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you get beat up?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were Jews. But we-- we really didn't think that this was that unusual.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name and what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Bela Krauss, and he was in business. He was a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Bertha Krauss, and she was a housewife. She was a housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was an only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you live at home with your mother and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I live at home?

speaker: interviewer
text: At home with your mother and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that ever change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father died. My-- my mother died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother died in 1935, and things changed, obviously. And my-- my father-- [PAUSES] my father-- [PAUSES] my father was the-- my father became the principal provider. And-- [PAUSES] so he was the principal provider, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay with your father at that time, or did you go anywhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I stayed with my father and my aunt. And-- [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your aunt's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt's name was Anna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you all lived together in one place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your aunt's husband there, or did they have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they had children, my cousin. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [? Micky. ?] And he was like my brother. And-- he was like my brother. [? Micky. ?] And-- [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, did you live in a house, or was it an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what it looked like? Remember anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Surely. It was-- it was a very comfortable apartment. And it was a cozy apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made it cozy?

speaker: interviewee
text: What made it cozy? Well, it was-- it was a cozy apartment. What made it cozy? It was-- it was-- it was a cozy apartment [LAUGHS].

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms did it have, if you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it had about seven rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the furniture like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we are talking about another apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. What was the furniture like? Typical middle European furniture.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color was it? Remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. It was-- [PAUSES] trying to describe it. [PAUSES] What color, did you ask me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a light-- light-- light-- [PAUSES] it was like a light beige. Light beige. And-- [PAUSES] it was a nice, cozy-- here I go again. But it was a cozy, cozy apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what kind of books there were in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We had lots of books. Both my aunts-- both my aunts, they were great readers. And I was a-- I was a good reader also. And-- [PAUSES] I remember I was-- I was reading a lot even as a child. I remember I was reading-- trying to think the books I was reading at that early age. [PAUSES] I was-- [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have religious books in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. But it wasn't-- it wasn't-- [PAUSES] it was not-- even though we had religious books, it wasn't the central part, even though that religion was an important part of-- but it was not-- can I have something to drink?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was I? You were stating that-- that you did have religious books in your house, as well as nonreligious books, but that it was not the central part of-- of life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of life. Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an important part of life but wasn't the central part of life. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that the family was observant of any rituals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the level of observance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Given a scale of from what to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, given the level of observances to Orthodox or Hasidic or-- or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to any particular temple that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We went to the Orthodox temple in my neighborhood. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what a typical Shabbos was like in that cozy apartment that you were talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a typical Shabbos was very warm. Typical Shabbos was-- [PAUSES] well, it was, as I said, very warm. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the table look like when you came home from shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: From shul, it was white tablecloths.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you usually eat on Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos, we had fish. We had-- [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewer
text: and we had-- we had-- [PAUSES] we had fish. We had-- [PAUSES] trying to think. You forget. We are talking about I am-- [PAUSES] I'm like-- I'm trying to think back. I was like-- how old was I now? Let me see. Young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Young. So how old was I? Let me see. Let me see that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in the beginning of the war, you were born in 1930. We're talking about just what you remember as a child, not-- not anything at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --wartime.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was young. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was school like? Did you go to a school that was a public school, or did you go to a private school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to a school which was a public school, but also a-- [PAUSES] it was a-- [PAUSES] public school. [PAUSES] I'm trying to think. It-- it was a public school, but mixed with a-- how should I do this-- how should I-- [PAUSES] it was mixed. [PAUSES] Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to school to this particular school, did you get along well with the-- those who were not Jewish? How did everybody interact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were all Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the children in your school were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And as far as-- how did they interact? Well, inasmuch as we were all Jewish, they interacted quite well. Right? They interacted quite well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you left school, did you ever have any incidents with any children who were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Yeah, sure, we-- we-- we did have-- we did have interactions. Not too severe, but we did have-- excuse me-- interactions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends who were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are many Jews in Budapest out of the entire population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Budapest had-- let me see. Budapest had-- [PAUSES] Budapest had a million-- Budapest had a million-- [PAUSES] oh, shit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had many Jews. Tell me, when you went to school, what was your favorite subject? What did you like the best?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trying to think. [PAUSES] I was too young to have really that many favorite subjects, when you think about it. I liked-- I liked-- [PAUSES] before, I liked-- [PAUSES] No. Writing. And it was-- it was-- yeah, I liked writing. I liked writing. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like to do in your spare time when you were home? Did you play with your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we played-- we played soccer. Normal boys' games.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things begin to change? When did you first feel a change?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first-- can I move this? The first big change was in 1930-- wait a second, 19-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] 19-- let me try to figure it out. 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- what happened that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1934, the-- the-- the Germans came in officially. They took-- they took over, let me see, 1934. 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: But without the date, what happened-- what happened to you? What did you-- what did you see that affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans came in, took over officially. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did it-- how did it affect your day-to-day life? Or did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure it did.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Day to day, every day there was a new rule-- rule or regulation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of rules that you-- that affected you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Affected me? It was rules-- rules and regulations were coming in as far as-- as far as-- [PAUSES] there were rules to the extent of-- it became like-- anti-Jewish laws became constant-- constant-- constant harassment. And so it was constant-- constant harassment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember hearing your-- your father, or your aunt, or your uncle talking about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. And sure we do. And I remember specifically my aunt would tell my uncle, don't spread rumors, you know, because we don't know what's going to happen yet, and blah, blah, blah, and this sort of stuff. And-- so don't forget. This period of time was-- [PAUSES] was like April, May, June, a very short time. It was a very short time, really

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to further change your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Further change your life?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- could you give some incidents of things that happened to you? What you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well. [PAUSES] Further changed my life. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any ghettos in-- in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have to be in that ghetto? Were you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto surrounded by any gates or anything? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they guarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was guarding them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if they were Hungarian guards, or German guards, or were there Jewish guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian and-- and German guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the ghetto that was set up that you had to be in near where you lived, or was it far from where you lived? Or was it where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was where I lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- did you ever have to leave the apartment that you were in to be in the ghetto? Or-- or were you still in the same apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to be-- we had to be in the same apartment, but we had to leave the ghetto. We had to leave the ghetto. How-- how-- how do you-- I don't know how you want to answer this. Well, you said--

speaker: interviewer
text: However you want. I'm just trying to see--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --if the apartment was in the-- in the confines of the ghetto. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: The apartment was in the confines of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you still stay-- was it-- how many-- was it still just you, your-- your aunt, your uncle, and your cousin in there? Or did other people come in-- into the apartment too? Did you have to share it with anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure, we had to share it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- who were the people? Were they also Hungarian from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember other people being brought in from other parts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever talk to them? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they coming and telling you any kind of stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what kind of stories were they telling you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horror stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe them? Were they believed by the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during this time? Did you have food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I have-- did we have food? You know, I am trying to think. Yes, we did have some hot food. We weren't starving. We did have some hot food. How? Search me. But we did have some hot food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your days like at that point? Were you going to school still?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was a typical day like for you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very boring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your cousin still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other children that lived with you in that-- in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you all do anything together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, played and-- it was very boring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear the grown-ups talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things were they talking about? What were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: What grownups talk about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the people that were in the ghetto-- did most of them have food, did you find? What-- what do you remember about the-- the goings on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I know we did have some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father with you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was taken to Auschwitz already.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change the tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
